attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: Books behind the tarot — sources + our editorial reading

name: Books Behind the Tarot — The Sources & Our Reading
description:
# Books Behind the Tarot

A grammar of the **sources** behind the cards — the occult-revival classics, the foundational texts, and the modern scholarship — each with **our editorial reading**: where it's reliable and where it overreaches. The twin of *People & Institutions of Tarot*. We don't host anything: **public-domain** works link to a free full text; **in-copyright** works are cited and linked, never reproduced.

items.name: Three Books of Occult Philosophy — Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa (1531 (Eng. 1651))

sections.What it is: The Renaissance encyclopedia of elements, planets, numbers and correspondences.
sections.Why it matters: Upstream of the whole Western correspondence system the occultists later mapped onto the cards.
sections.Our editorial view: Foundational PD source — but predates tarot's occult use entirely. Context for the correspondences, **not a tarot book**.
sections.Copyright: **Public domain.** Free to read, quote, and cite.

items.name: Le Monde Primitif, vol. VIII — Antoine Court de Gébelin (1781)

sections.What it is: The essay that first claimed the tarot was ancient Egyptian wisdom.
sections.Why it matters: The single text that launched tarot's occult reinterpretation.
sections.Our editorial view: Beautifully written and historically **wrong** — the birthplace of the Egyptian myth. Read it as the start of meaning *added*, not as history.
sections.Copyright: **Public domain.** Free to read, quote, and cite.

sections.What it is: The first systematic method for divining with tarot, and the first purpose-made divinatory deck.
sections.Why it matters: Turned Court de Gébelin's theory into a practice; the root of modern card reading.
sections.Our editorial view: Genuinely first at *cartomancy* — but his Egyptian/Thoth origin claims are invention. Credit the practice, flag the pseudo-history.
sections.Copyright: **Public domain.** Free to read, quote, and cite.

items.name: Dogme et Rituel de la Haute Magie — Éliphas Lévi (1854–56)

sections.What it is: The work that welded the 22 trumps to the 22 Hebrew letters and the Kabbalah.
sections.Why it matters: The hinge between French occultism and the later Golden Dawn / RWS systems.
sections.Our editorial view: Hugely influential, intellectually serious — and the tarot-Kabbalah link is **his construction**, not a historical inheritance.
sections.Copyright: **Public domain.** Free to read, quote, and cite.

items.name: “Book T” — The Tarot (Golden Dawn) — S. L. MacGregor Mathers (Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn) (c. 1888)

metadata.author: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn)

sections.What it is: The Golden Dawn's internal tarot paper: decans, sephiroth, elemental dignities, the “Lord of…” minor titles.
sections.Why it matters: The actual source our golden-dawn-book-t deck is built on, and the system RWS visualizes.
sections.Our editorial view: The **primary document** to cite (PD; Mathers d. 1918) — not Regardie's copyrighted 1937/1984 reprints. Use it to verify our deck's correspondences.
sections.Copyright: **Public domain.** Free to read, quote, and cite.

items.name: Le Tarot des Bohémiens — Papus (Gérard Encausse) (1889)

sections.What it is: A systematizing occult treatise tying tarot to Kabbalah and 'the Bohemians' (Roma).
sections.Why it matters: Spread Lévi's synthesis to a wider audience around 1900.
sections.Our editorial view: Erudite and confident — and the **Roma-origin claim is a myth**. Period piece, not evidence.
sections.Copyright: **Public domain.** Free to read, quote, and cite.

items.name: The Pictorial Key to the Tarot — Arthur Edward Waite (1911)

sections.What it is: Waite's companion to the Rider-Waite-Smith deck — card-by-card meanings.
sections.Why it matters: The most influential divinatory text of the last century; defines the 'standard' RWS readings.
sections.Our editorial view: Now public domain (Waite d. 1942). Authoritative *for RWS*, but Waite mystifies on purpose and downplays Pamela Colman Smith's art.
sections.Copyright: **Public domain.** Free to read, quote, and cite.

items.name: The Game of Tarot — Michael Dummett (1980)

sections.What it is: The foundational scholarly history: tarot as a 15th-c. Italian card game.
sections.Why it matters: The book that put tarot history on rigorous, game-first footing.
sections.Our editorial view: Our north star for the **game-first** thesis. In copyright — cite and link, don't host.
sections.Copyright: **In copyright** — link and cite only; we don't host it.

items.name: A Wicked Pack of Cards — Ronald Decker, Thierry Depaulis & Michael Dummett (1996)

metadata.author: Ronald Decker, Thierry Depaulis & Michael Dummett

sections.What it is: The definitive history of the *occult* tarot — how the esoteric myths were invented, by whom.
sections.Why it matters: Documents exactly where Court de Gébelin, Etteilla & co. went wrong.
sections.Our editorial view: Our primary authority for debunking origin myths. In copyright — reference only.
sections.Copyright: **In copyright** — link and cite only; we don't host it.

items.name: A Cultural History of Tarot — Helen Farley (2009)

sections.What it is: An accessible scholarly survey from playing card to esoteric tool.
sections.Why it matters: A readable bridge between Dummett-level rigor and the general reader.
sections.Our editorial view: Solid modern synthesis. In copyright — cite and link.
sections.Copyright: **In copyright** — link and cite only; we don't host it.
sections.Where to read: In print (I.B. Tauris, 2009) — find it via your library or a bookseller.

items.name: The Book of Thoth — Aleister Crowley (1944)

sections.What it is: Crowley's esoteric system for the Thoth deck painted by Lady Frieda Harris.
sections.Why it matters: The most elaborate 20th-c. occult tarot synthesis.
sections.Our editorial view: Brilliant and idiosyncratic. **In copyright** (text, and Harris's images to ~2033) — reference, never reproduce. See our note on the Thoth deck being off-limits.
sections.Copyright: **Jurisdiction-split.** Crowley's *text* is public domain in life+70 countries (he died 1947 → since 2018), but likely still protected in the **US (~2039)**, and Lady Frieda Harris's *paintings* stay in copyright to **~2033**. We don't host it; where the text is PD for you, a PD scan may be findable, but the deck imagery is not free.

items.name: The Tarot: History, Symbolism, and Divination — Robert M. Place (2005)

sections.What it is: A practitioner-scholar's synthesis of history and symbolism.
sections.Why it matters: Bridges the historical record and the divinatory tradition fairly.
sections.Our editorial view: Balanced and well-sourced for a popular book. In copyright — cite and link.
sections.Copyright: **In copyright** — link and cite only; we don't host it.

items.name: The Encyclopedia of Tarot (Vols I–IV) — Stuart R. Kaplan (1978–2005)

sections.What it is: The standard multi-volume catalog of tarot decks and their imagery.
sections.Why it matters: The most comprehensive reference catalog of decks ever assembled.
sections.Our editorial view: Indispensable as a **catalog** — but descriptive and collector-focused; pair it with Dummett for critical history.
sections.Copyright: **In copyright** — link and cite only; we don't host it.
sections.Where to read: In print (1978–2005) — find it via your library or a bookseller.

items.name: A History of the Occult Tarot — Ronald Decker & Michael Dummett (2002)

sections.What it is: The sequel to *A Wicked Pack of Cards*, tracing the occult tarot 1870–1970.
sections.Why it matters: Completes the rigorous history of how the esoteric tarot actually developed.
sections.Our editorial view: With *Wicked Pack*, our backbone for the occult-tarot story. In copyright — cite and link.
sections.Copyright: **In copyright** — link and cite only; we don't host it.

items.name: A History of Games Played with the Tarot Pack — Michael Dummett & John McLeod (2004)

sections.What it is: The definitive survey of every tarot **game**, country by country.
sections.Why it matters: The source for how the cards were actually played — what our playable games rest on.
sections.Our editorial view: The authority on tarot *as games* — the lineage behind our Tarocchino, Trionfi & Ma Diao reconstructions.
sections.Copyright: **In copyright** — link and cite only; we don't host it.

items.name: Secrets of the Waite-Smith Tarot — Marcus Katz & Tali Goodwin (2015)

sections.What it is: A deep study of the Rider-Waite-Smith deck's creation, sources, and Pamela Colman Smith.
sections.Why it matters: The best modern scholarship on the most influential deck's making.
sections.Our editorial view: Essential for the RWS deck — and it restores **Pamela Colman Smith's** authorship. In copyright.
sections.Copyright: **In copyright** — link and cite only; we don't host it.
sections.Where to read: In print (2015) — find it via your library or a bookseller.

items.name: Tarot: The Library of Esoterica — Jessica Hundley (ed.) (2020)

sections.What it is: A lavish visual cultural history of tarot imagery across traditions.
sections.Why it matters: A wide, beautiful survey of the cards as art.
sections.Our editorial view: Gorgeous image survey — curation over critical history; enjoy the pictures, verify claims elsewhere.
sections.Copyright: **In copyright** — link and cite only; we don't host it.
sections.Where to read: In print (2020) — find it via your library or a bookseller.

items.name: The Complete Golden Dawn System of Magic — Israel Regardie (1984)

sections.What it is: The compendium of Golden Dawn rituals, knowledge lectures, and tarot (it reprints Book T).
sections.Why it matters: The most accessible modern reprint of the Golden Dawn material that shaped occult tarot.
sections.Our editorial view: Convenient compendium — but for the tarot primary, cite **Book T** (public domain), not this copyrighted reprint.
sections.Copyright: **In copyright** — link and cite only; we don't host it.
